title: Biweekly change in confirmed COVID-19 cases
subtitle: The biweekly change on any given date measures the percentage change in the number of new confirmed cases over the last 14 days, relative to the number in the previous 14 days. Due to limited testing, the number of confirmed cases is lower than the true number of infections.
caption:
Biweekly change in confirmed COVID-19 cases

The biweekly change on any given date measures the percentage change in the number of new confirmed cases over the last 14 days, relative to the number in the previous 14 days. Due to limited testing, the number of confirmed cases is lower than the true number of infections.

This choropleth world map displays the biweekly change in confirmed COVID-19 cases as of February 22, 2026. The legend spans from -100% to +100%, with color bands marking every 25 percentage points: dark blue for -100% to -75%, medium blue for -75% to -50%, light blue for -50% to -25%, very light blue for -25% to 0%, pale orange for 0% to 25%, medium orange for 25% to 50%, darker orange for 50% to 75%, and deep red for 75% to 100%. Countries shaded in diagonal hatching indicate no data. Peru and several Central American nations including Guatemala and Honduras fall into the darkest blue band, indicating a decline of 75% to 100% in cases over the prior two weeks. Argentina and parts of Eastern Europe including Romania and Bulgaria appear in medium to light blue shades, corresponding to declines between 25% and 75%. Brazil and Sweden are shaded in pale orange, placing them in the 0% to 25% increase range. Most of Africa, Asia, Australia, and North America show hatched patterns, meaning no data is reported for those regions. A few countries in South America and Europe display mid-range blue tones, suggesting moderate declines. The visual distribution shows concentrated case reductions in parts of Latin America and Eastern Europe, while large portions of the globe lack reported figures. The color scale allows direct comparison of relative changes across reporting nations, with darker blues signaling sharper drops and warmer tones indicating growth or stability in case counts.
